speaker: interviewer
text: It's October 4, 1996. This is an interview with Mrs. Thea Rumstein. My name is Brad Kurtzberg. We're in Levittown, New York, USA. And the interview will be conducted in English. It's October 4, 1996. This is an interview with Mrs. Thea Rumstein, maiden name Gottesmann. My name is Brad Kurtzberg. We're in Levittown, New York, USA. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Mrs. Rumstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please state and spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Thea, T-H-E-A, Rumstein. R-U-M-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My name was Thea Gottesmann. G-O-T-T-E-S-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna, Austria, February 6, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your childhood in Vienna before the Nazis came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was 10 years old when Hitler came. So what I remember is that we had a pretty normal childhood, my brother and I, my father, and my mother. We lived in the outer district, but we moved in closer to where my grandparents and my aunt and uncles lived, so we could see them more often and they could babysit for us. And we went every Friday to my grandfather for [NON-ENGLISH] Shabbat. Because my parents were not religious. They were Jewish. And we knew that we were Jewish. But they were not religious. I always wanted to be, but I had to look for that in-- at my grandfather's house. And I went to school. And every Sunday we-- they brought us to the movies. And like, we had a pretty normal childhood. The only thing is, I was aware when I was-- I-- I understood that there was something going on even before '38. That there were some kind of fights always in Austria, between the right radicals and the socialists. And this was already influenced through Germany. It was already-- they tried already making all kinds of troubles. As a matter of fact, in 1934, the chancellor of Austria, [NON-ENGLISH], was shot by the right radicals. And really, that was already 1934 and I was six years old. And I remember that. I remember people on the streets running, and shooting. And afterwards, the houses were shot. Even, as a matter of fact, when I went back to Vienna, there still could see the bullet holes in these particular houses. But nevertheless, you know, we were children. And we played on the street. And we played at home. And we visited the amusement parks. And it was pretty good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned spending Friday nights with your grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: First, what was your grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yakob. Yakob.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this your mother's father or your father's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's father, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what would these Friday nights be like? What-- what did the two of you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was his favorite. He had-- we had my brother, and then I had another cousin, who unfortunately got also killed. [? Willy ?] was his name. And like I said, my mother's sisters lived now in the same district. And, well, we got there. First of all, the food was very important. You know, we had chicken. We had fish and chicken and prunes and what do you call that, this-- what you eat, this thing that you eat, with-- with gefilte fish. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, and of course, the praying and-- and the candle lighting, that-- that I always liked. And I didn't have it at home but I-- I started it at home myself. Because I-- I just always felt, we have to, you know, identify with someone. And, but we-- they were get-togethers and they were always happy ones, happy get-togethers. You mentioned your immediate family. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Julius. Actually, it was Jyula. It's a Hungarian name. J-Y-U-L-A. But in Austria, it's translated into Julius.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a pharmacist. And he worked in a pharmacy. And that was his profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What was your father like? Do you have any special--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --memories of him? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. He was-- well, like, everybody would say that. But he was a wonderful father. Was very good-looking. He was tall and very charming. And jovial. Always joking with other people and with us. And I have very good memories of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about a specific time you spent with your father that stands out in your mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I mentioned, every Sunday-- you know, in Vienna, it was like a routine. First we were-- my brother and I went to the movies, to the children's performance. And afterwards-- that was, of course, in the summer and when good weathers. And afterwards, there's a very famous amusement park in Vienna that's called the Prater. And so-- and they have also gardens there, and-- and coffee houses. And so we used to walk there and went on all these amusement merry-go-rounds and whatever. And these-- that's the time we spent with him. Otherwise, he was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was home with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gusti. G-U-S-T-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And tell me something about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- again, she was great. She was really the rock of the-- of the family, especially after my father didn't come back. She was very, very musical, had a beautiful voice. As a matter of fact, she wanted to become a singer. But my grandparents, especially my grandfather, was pretty strict. And I thought, to go into show business wouldn't be-- for a Jewish girl. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: But she was always singing. And that instilled a lot of music in me, too. Because instead of singing lullabies, she sang operas to us. You know? Like [INAUDIBLE], whatever, [INAUDIBLE]. Things that-- that come very easy. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a favorite that you remember? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Opera?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a book here that when she went to the opera and she crossed off all the operas. I think there's none that she hasn't seen, that she-- and like I said, well, I'll tell you later, when we started going to the opera, right when we came back. And she was a wonderful mother. She learned how to sew. She was-- actually, that was her profession. And she was a very good-- she's not even a seamstress. She was-- she went to Berlin and she worked in a-- a big company there, as a designer, or whatever. And so she sewed for us, too. Whatever had to be fixed and whatever had to be done, she did for us. And she was not a great baker. [LAUGHTER] We went to good bakeries. She-- but she was a good-- good mother. Excellent. And-- and wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mention you had a brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. George. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was exactly a year and six days older than I am. So we really grew up like twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the two of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We got dressed alike. You know? And we-- we got along wonderful. He was-- I was more the aggressive part. And he was more-- he teased me and then I became aggressive. [LAUGHTER] But-- but we had-- we had a wonderful relationship, as children, especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your education? What-- what kind of schooling did you get in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, I was 10. So I was in fourth grade. I'd just started fourth grade when-- when Hitler came. And I was in the-- I was in a mixed school, of course, which didn't last too long. In the beginning, you know, we-- we even-- I remember, the first couple of days, we had to write swastikas, you know, in a row. And it said, March 13, our-- our leader, fuehrer Adolf Hitler came. I mean, as a Jewish child, I-- we had to do this. But this didn't last long. Because, first of all, they started already [? haunting ?] us. And they spit at us, and-- the kids who knew that we were Jewish. And we-- we stayed, I think, a few months. And then we had to get out of school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Before Hitler came to power in Austria--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how would you describe relations between Jews and Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we didn't know the difference. We really didn't know the difference. We had-- I had girlfriends. And I must say, there were quite of a lot of non-Jewish. Which, of course, as soon as Hitler came in, especially one, I was very close with her. And when Hitler came in, maybe the same week, she says Thea, you cannot come and visit anymore. Because my father doesn't let me talk to you. My father doesn't allow me. For me, this was kind of, you know-- especially her, because she lived very close by. And I had no idea. But otherwise, you know, I was-- I, like I said, there was-- I remember also there were a lot of poor Jewish children in our class. We-- we used to drink milk, you know, we-- everybody bought milk. And everybody left a little bit milk left in in the bottle. And we poured it in the other one so to make a full one again for another child, so that they should have the [INAUDIBLE], because you had to pay for this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and didn't get it for free. Well, I loved to go to school. I was-- [LAUGHS] I always liked to get all-- all As, always, in-- in class. And you know, you're a child. You really didn't-- didn't know the difference. Later on, I heard from my parents that-- you know, that they-- from the people that they hung out with, friends who are not Jewish, who actually were illegal Nazis. And they told them, they said, they told them and predicted that this is going to come. I'm talking just a-- a month or so before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, because everything was brewing to the point, in Austria. They said, you-- you should leave here. You should leave here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we said, where should we go? Where should you-- can you, like, pick yourself up with two little kids, and where are you going to go? So, but basically, with us, with us children, we-- we had a normal childhood, you know? When the Nazis took power, you mentioned you weren't allowed to go to the public school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --shortly thereafter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other changes took place for you and your family that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were tremendous changes. Tremendous changes. I must say, I remember-- March-- first of all, I told you there were things before already. There were supposed to be elections in Austria, you know? And they put all kinds of-- of signs on the streets. They painted the streets to-- to vote yes for the-- for-- not for Hitler, for the other one. And as you know, they didn't even come out. Because Hitler marched in without waiting for-- for the-- for the election. So, of course we are-- I know my parents, I remember very well, and so did I, that we were glued to the-- to the radio-- what was going on. And when the Germans-- you know, the-- the Austrians welcomed them very well. If anybody says that they were overrun or they were-- you know, that they didn't want Hitler, or I wasn't for Hitler, they are lying. In general, and their-- majority-- I'm sure there were people who-- because nobody really knew what is going to follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, the majority of people welcomed him. Welcomed him with open hands and open hearts. You know? And, so we saw them starting marching, marching on the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, the next day, all the stores had already signs-- Juden [NON-ENGLISH]. Not-- Jews are not wanted here. And the Jewish stores, where-- where-- [INAUDIBLE], you know, Jude, they wrote on them then. And all kinds of things happened. There was a lot of things going on. And you were sitting there and you didn't really know what is going to happen. And so when we found out, it was terrible. The first thing is-- that I remember very well-- when I came home from school-- that was just in the beginning-- I went on the street where we lived. And I-- there was a whole bunch of people standing around, and, you know, shouting, and shouting, Juden, and so on and so forth. And here I see my mother, on the street, on her feet, cleaning the street. They took her to clean up the-- the signs that they-- that they put on the streets for the election.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, Ma, what are you doing here? And she says, Thea, go home. Go home. She didn't want me to stand there. And I cried tremendously.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and I didn't go home. I stayed there. I was like, Mommy. And the people, they had no compassion whatsoever. They were full of hate. All of them. All of them. I guess who were not, were not standing there. But, as a-- as a matter of fact, when we went to [NON-ENGLISH], I saw a picture of Austria, where people are standing around. And-- and I always-- I always imagine this is my mother. She looked like her. I mean, she might not be her. But it is a very, very well-known picture. And I saw it in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did that make you feel when you saw your mother like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was-- terrible. Terrible. Unhappy. I was afraid. That's when I started being afraid. And I was afraid to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother talk to you about this when she got home? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she say?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came home. But you know what it is? I think that people, all the time, lived in a kind of a denial. Because my parents knew already, from people who came from-- from Germany, there were people who ran away from Germany, who told stories. I mean, it wasn't-- it really started in Germany in '33. But the real bad time, also in Germany, started in '38, when-- when Austria was taken over. And I remember my parents telling us, and-- and I-- I remember, even, people-- and you know, you always thought, oh, maybe-- maybe they were not good. Maybe they were-- only the Polish people there were persecuting, not the-- not the Germans. And maybe they did something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? You lived in an egg shell of denial. And, well, as a child, I couldn't do anything about it anyhow. And that-- then all things started to happen after '38. What else started to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, we lived on a main street, main street where people were marching, and the soldiers, and the people-- Jews were not allowed to live on a main street. So we had to move out where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a side street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street did you live on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived on-- in the Heine Strasse. Like, Heine, the famous poem-- poet. And from there, we had to move, it's called [GERMAN]. It doesn't mean-- only somebody from Vienna might know. We moved into a very large apartment. But this didn't belong to us. We had to share it with, I think it was four or five other families. Everybody get-- got a room. We had two rooms. Two rooms. One where my brother and I slept and where my parents slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was one kitchen for all the people. And I remember, there was only one bathroom for all the people. But we somehow managed, you know? We made the best out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived there. And you know, my parents were, at that time, they were young. And we had a lot of people came to visit us and they tried to make it as normal-- as normal as possible. And wait, what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you know these other four families previously to when they moved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't. But it turned out-- which was very lucky for me-- don't forget, there were a lot of educated people there. This one was a professor. And-- a German professor-- Dr. Kornitzer, with his wife. And there another one was a math-- math professor, with his wife. Forgot his name. But somehow, while I was there and I couldn't go to school, I always ask him to give me something to-- to-- to learn. And-- and I took a little math from him and a little German from them. And-- and, you know. And we were discussing things. As a matter of fact, Professor Kornitzer was a great help to us when I came back. Don't forget, I was already 17 years old. And I had no formal schooling. But he vouched for me so that I could make-- could go on and go for higher education. And he said, she will-- she'll be able to catch up. So I got-- you know, I got into a class so I could go on with my education after the war, because of the-- Professor Kornitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: In between when you were kicked out of the public school, when you couldn't attend there anymore, and being taught by these professors--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --apartment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was there any schooling in between?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. This is the-- at-- that-- I got-- first, from-- when we got thrown out from the school, there was a school, it's called [NON-ENGLISH] in a second district, which was later a gathering camp for the-- for us to send us away. But before, it was a school. And all the Jewish children from all over Vienna had to go to this school. But also, not for long, because that also was converted into a camp. And we had to-- we had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in that school? Did-- what did you learn about?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, you see, in a way, all the best teachers were Jewish teachers. And so we had very good teachers in that particular school. The only thing is, it didn't last long. And-- but we got kind of a little bit-- I mean, 10 years, 11, 12 years. And that was it. After 12, I didn't go to school anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the school was, you know, we knew what was going on. We knew we was tainted by the people. I remember they stood in front, the non-Jewish children, you know, in the winter, when we had a cap on, a hat on, for me, and I was-- they tore it off me. And you know. And they run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and, I mean, they-- we were constantly haunted by the-- by-- by the children. Because they heard it at home, and they-- the Jews are no good. And then we were one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what other changes occurred for you and your family at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the changes were that we, like I said, we could-- nobody could go to work. Nobody had-- you know, we had-- well, this went on until, let's say, 1940. We couldn't go-- and then we had, I think in 1939, the war started with Poland. Hitler went into Poland. And then, England and France declared war. So the war started already in '39. And then he went to Czechoslovakia and to France and to all over. And I remember they were all the store-- first of all, flags all over, all the time. You know, the-- the swastikas, all over. And then we had to get the Jewish star. Then we really couldn't go anyplace, because then we had to be-- we are identified as being Jewish. I remember sitting on a windowsill and watching the people go by and be terrible, terrible envious about every-- everything that I saw, that I couldn't go anyplace. Couldn't play outside. They also started very much harassing us. Us and the whole-- the whole-- all the Jews. Because in the winter, they came and picked up my father and my mother, and then us, too, to shovel the snow. We had to shovel snow for hours, for hours. And the people were standing around and laughing. I remember I had a pair of boots. And they wanted-- they didn't take it. But they said, we could use those boots. You know, we-- [INAUDIBLE] would use those boots. But they didn't take 'em away. But I was afraid I will have to go barefoot in the snow. And all in all, the people were hateful. Absolutely hateful. And the one who were not, were not being-- we couldn't hear them. We couldn't see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one way, they were afraid. If you-- you know? Because we had a maid from before-- maid, somebody who cleaned and helped clean-- and she couldn't talk to us. I remember she came sometimes at night, you know, and brought us something. And then-- you know, all at night, so that nobody could see her. It was terrible times. Very terrible. And it-- it-- it went, it got worse and worse. And of course, then we had the Kristallnacht, you know? When the synagogues were burned.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was November 9th. They say it's 10th. I remember November 9th. No. November 10th. There is a dispute when it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939. Then the Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we had a synagogue very close by, the [NON-ENGLISH] synagogue. It's called [NON-ENGLISH] temple. I saw it going up in flame. It went up. It was a very, very well-known synagogue. And we went to this synagogue. And all the people from the neighborhood went to this synagogue. And it went up. It mean, there were other-- a few others in the-- in the district. But this was a really-- we could see it. Because it was in-- in-- very close to us. And again, the people were happy and laughing that-- that this can happen. Nobody prevented it.

speaker: interviewer
text: As these things started to happen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what do you remember the reaction of your parents being?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were very worried. And there were often talks, what are we going to do. I had relatives in Hungary, since my father was from Budapest. His two sisters and his mother lived in Budapest. And there were people who went illegally over the border, you know? There is a big lake between Austria and Hungary. And it's called [NON-ENGLISH]. It's large. But it's a-- a-- a-- sea-- a lake that is not very deep. And people could wade through there, with-- with somebody who took them. And I remember, one night, we were already ready to go there. But somehow, the last minute, the guy didn't show up who was supposed to-- I think my father gave him, even, money. Didn't show up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we-- we stayed. We didn't go. And as it turned out, it wouldn't have made any difference. Because my relatives in Hungary were just as much caught as we, you know, because Hitler went there too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were people who got affidavits to go to the United States. And we got one from a distant cousin of my mother's. But my father, since he was born in Hungary, didn't have the right quota. The quota-- we-- we had to wait. And that's the reason we didn't-- we couldn't come here. So we stayed in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your parents being worried and trying to find a way to get out. Do you remember any kind of group response from other Jews or the organized Jewish community? Any rabbis or any leaders in the community saying anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was nothing to say. Everybody was afraid. It was-- it-- it-- you-- there the Jewish leaders were kind of silenced because you were nothing. You became nothing. There was no more Jewish leaders. There was called the [NON-ENGLISH], where they have, like, the organization of-- of the Jews who took care of the cemeteries and all this things. And I don't know. I guess people used to pay dues to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it ended. '38 was-- and '39, and then everybody was dispersed. People tried to get out. People to-- to-- to-- tried to just hope that the world will-- is not going to let it happen, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially my father was the biggest optimist. He says, no way will he get away with this. But he did. And-- no. We just lived our life from one day to the other. What is going to happen. First, one time is that my-- we were supposed to go to England, my brother and I, to Glasgow. To Scotland. And they tried to initiate that. And then the [INAUDIBLE]-- the last thing was, again, look. We're staying together. Whatever happens to us, will happen to all of us. They couldn't-- we couldn't part. Because we were children. And I guess my parents didn't have the heart to-- to just-- [INAUDIBLE] it was just initiated. It was not actually happening yet. But we didn't pursue it. Because my father said, my mother said, whatever happen, we'll stay together. Not knowing what's going to happen anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. At this point, after the-- after the Nazis came in, from '38 till '40, roughly, were you still able to to celebrate Shabbat with your grandfather? Was there any kind of Jewish observance still able to go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. In the beginning, yes, I would say. Because like I said, we just walked over to him. And we had-- we might not have had the same food or something, but-- in the beginning. I'm talking, now, '38, '39. '40, it started already with the deportations. '41 was the biggest deportations.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to stop here, tape number one, and pick this up with tape number two.
